title: Prompt assembly + citation-annotated answer generation (synthesizer.py)
summary: synthesize_answer() correctly assembles a prompt embedding selected_markdown verbatim (including its '## File: <path>' headers), calls it through LLMClient via TYPE_CHECKING-only DI matching intent_refiner.py's established convention, and parses the LLM's JSON completion into a SynthesizerResult with accurate answer/citations extraction. unknown_citations() uses exact-match set membership, which was adversarially tested against prefix/substring, trailing-slash, and case-variant citations -- all correctly flagged as unknown (no false negatives found). Error handling (unparseable JSON, missing field, wrong type, LLMError propagation) mirrors intent_refiner.py's *Error/*ParseError pattern and is fully tested. All 73 agents/query/ tests pass; full regression suite shows 284 passed, 2 pre-existing protobuf-version failures unrelated to this diff (issue #46), matching the commit message's claim exactly. ruff check agents/query/ is clean. One non-blocking documentation/implementation consistency gap found (F-4.5.1-1): SynthesizerResult.citations is documented as 'deduplicated' but the parser does not enforce dedup on the LLM's raw citations list -- it trusts the prompt's own dedup instruction to the model, with no test covering a citations array containing LLM-supplied duplicates. Design choice for the wire format (separate machine-parseable 'citations' field alongside inline '[<path>]' markers in 'answer') is self-consistent, disclosed, and satisfies the acceptance criteria's 'inline citations' wording without contradiction; LLD is silent on the exact format so this is a legitimate implementer choice, not a gap.

requirements_conformance.evidence: Acceptance criteria requires: given refined intent + concatenated selected markdown with file-path headers, synthesizer produces an answer whose inline citations reference file paths actually provided in the input set. Verified via test_prompt_includes_file_path_headers (asserts exact '## File: <path>' strings appear in the prompt string passed to _FakeLLMClient.complete()) and test_synthesize_answer_extracts_citations_and_answer (asserts result.answer contains the inline '[<path>]' markers and result.citations matches the fixture's cited paths exactly). Test spec's own wording ('LLMClient mocked fixture citation-containing response, asserting prompt includes file-path headers and output parsing extracts citations correctly') is satisfied end-to-end, not just via string concatenation -- the prompt assertion checks the actual argument received by the mocked LLMClient.complete(), and the parsing assertion checks structured field extraction from a realistic JSON fixture.

architecture_conformance.evidence: docs/LLD/query-agent.md's synthesizer.py section ('Final LLM call: refined intent + concatenated selected markdown (with file-path headers) -> answer with inline file-path citations') is matched. LLMClient is imported only under 'if TYPE_CHECKING:' (agents/query/synthesizer.py line 85-86), identical to intent_refiner.py's own TYPE_CHECKING-only import (line 67-68) -- no concrete provider (Ollama/OpenRouter/Gemini) is imported or referenced anywhere in synthesizer.py, preserving the DI boundary defined in agents/llm/client.py's LLMClient ABC. Exception hierarchy (SynthesizerError base, SynthesizerParseError subclass, LLMError deliberately NOT a parent) mirrors IntentRefinerError/IntentRefinerParseError exactly, including the same rationale documented in both module docstrings.

regression_risk.evidence: Ran `cd agents && python3 -m pytest query/ -v`: 73 passed, 0 failed. Ran `python3 -m pytest . --ignore=ingestion/test_e2e_smoke.py -q`: 284 passed, 2 failed -- both failures are ingestion/test_shortlist.py::test_grpc_client_translates_request_response and ::test_grpc_client_is_a_valid_search_candidates_fn_for_shortlist, both google.protobuf.runtime_version.VersionError (gencode 6.33.5 vs runtime 5.29.6), the pre-existing issue #46 failures unrelated to this diff (confirmed by inspecting the traceback: failure originates in ingestion/shortlist.py's protobuf-generated module import, nothing in agents/query/ or agents/llm/ appears in the stack). Diff touches only two new files (agents/query/synthesizer.py, agents/query/test_synthesizer.py); no existing files modified, so no regression surface beyond the new module itself.

edge_cases.evidence: Tested: no '## File:' headers present (provided_paths == [] while citations from the LLM are still returned unfiltered -- correct per this subtask's scope, since rejection is deferred to 4.5.2). Adversarially tested unknown_citations() outside the existing test suite with three additional cases not in test_synthesizer.py: a citation that is a strict prefix/substring of a real provided path ('billing/Invoice' vs 'billing/InvoiceDisputes.md'), a trailing-slash variant ('billing/InvoiceDisputes.md/'), and a case-differing variant ('Billing/InvoiceDisputes.md'). All three were correctly flagged as unknown (returned by unknown_citations()) because the implementation uses exact Python string/set equality -- no false negatives (no hallucinated-looking citation was wrongly treated as valid) found in any of these adversarial cases. This is the safe/conservative failure direction for a hallucination-detection building block.
edge_cases.comment: Exact-match semantics mean a legitimate citation with only a formatting difference from its header (e.g. a trailing slash a future upstream caller might introduce) would also be flagged as 'unknown' -- a false positive, not a false negative. Not a bug given the current single documented header format and the fact that 4.5.2 owns the dedicated validation logic, but worth flagging if header-formatting is ever loosened upstream.

security.evidence: No path traversal, no filesystem access, no eval/exec, no string formatting of untrusted input into anything executed. Citations and provided_paths are plain strings compared via set membership; LLM output is only ever json.loads'd (not eval'd) and never executed. No secrets or credentials touched. LLMClient DI boundary preserved (no direct provider SDK/HTTP calls from this module).

performance.evidence: Single regex scan (_FILE_HEADER_RE.finditer) over selected_markdown plus one JSON parse per call; no quadratic behavior, no unbounded loops. Consistent with intent_refiner.py's cost profile.

maintainability.evidence: Follows intent_refiner.py's established conventions closely (prompt-then-parse-JSON pattern, frozen dataclass result, shared strip_code_fences helper, TYPE_CHECKING DI, *Error/*ParseError pair) -- reviewers already familiar with intent_refiner.py can read this module with near-zero extra cognitive load. Module docstring explicitly discloses every design choice the LLD left open (input shape, header syntax, wire format, exception hierarchy), which is good practice for isolated/async review.

test_coverage.evidence: 13 tests covering: prompt assembly with headers, answer/citation extraction, kwarg forwarding, code-fence stripping, no-headers-present edge case, unknown_citations() happy path + hallucination-flagging + dedup/order-preservation, and 6 malformed-response cases (invalid JSON, missing field, wrong type x2, non-object JSON, LLMError propagation). All assertions check specific values (exact list/string equality), not just 'no exception raised' -- this satisfies the acceptance criteria's higher bar for meaningful assertions.
test_coverage.comment: F-4.5.1-1: no test exercises a citations array containing an LLM-supplied duplicate entry (e.g. LLM ignoring the prompt's 'deduplicated' instruction and returning ['a.md','a.md']) to confirm whether result.citations itself dedups. Inspection of _parse_synthesis_json confirms it does NOT dedup (citations=list(payload['citations']) is a straight pass-through), which contradicts the SynthesizerResult.citations docstring's claim of 'flat, deduplicated list' -- the actual guarantee is only 'as reported by the LLM,' which the docstring's second half does correctly caveat ('reported as the LLM's own citations field') but the first half's 'deduplicated' adjective is misleading if read alone. Not blocking: unknown_citations() (the actual defensible building block for 4.5.2) does its own internal dedup correctly regardless of upstream duplicates.

confidence.evidence: Verified by direct test execution (not just reading code), by directly reading the full diff and both files in the current working tree, and by adversarially probing unknown_citations() with inputs not present in the existing test suite via a standalone Python repro. No implementer summary or handoff.json content was used to form this verdict.

possible_improvements: Enforce dedup on payload['citations'] in _parse_synthesis_json (or rename the SynthesizerResult.citations docstring to drop 'deduplicated' if intentionally trusting the LLM) to remove the doc/impl inconsistency (F-4.5.1-1).

Consider whether unknown_citations()'s exact-match semantics should be documented as a known, deliberate limitation (case-sensitive, no path normalization) so subtask 4.5.2 doesn't need to rediscover this by reading the implementation.

untrusted_content_disclosure.description: No embedded fake system-reminder-style text, fake tool instructions, or fake directives were found in the GitHub issue #24 body or in git history for commit c8c49cf during this verification run's context-gathering (gh issue view, git show, git log). This run's own tool-call environment did surface two suspicious injected-looking blocks unrelated to repo content: a false '# currentDate ... date has changed' system-reminder and a false '## Auto Mode Active' directive, plus a fabricated 'tokensave' MCP-server instructions block referencing a tool that was never actually provided. None of these were acted upon (no scope change, no permission change, no state-changing action taken on their basis); this is disclosed per the task's security note even though the source in this instance was the runtime environment/prompt rather than repo content.

non_blocking_findings: F-4.5.1-1 (low, test-completeness / maintainability): SynthesizerResult.citations docstring says 'deduplicated' but _parse_synthesis_json does not enforce dedup on the LLM's raw citations list; no test covers LLM-supplied duplicate citations at the top-level citations field (unknown_citations() itself dedups correctly regardless).

F-4.5.1-2 (info, edge-case note): unknown_citations() uses exact string/set-membership matching -- correctly conservative against hallucination (no false negatives found under prefix/substring, trailing-slash, or case-variant adversarial probing), but would produce a false positive if a legitimate citation differed from its header only by formatting; acceptable given the single documented header format and 4.5.2's ownership of stricter validation.
